nodes.description: Long-term or prophylactic antibiotics to control respiratory infections.

evidence.reference_title: Bacteriology and treatment of infections in the upper and lower airways in patients with primary ciliary dyskinesia: adressing the paranasal sinuses.
evidence.snippet: Non-functional airway cilia impair the mucociliary clearance (MCC), causing mucostasis, lung infections and destruction, chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) and hearing impairment. It is of paramount importance to postpone chronic lung infection mainly with Gram-negative bacteria (GNB) in patients with an impaired MCC. When successful, lung function can be stabilized and quality of life (QoL) improved.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment recommendations in Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia.
evidence.snippet: Most of the treatments recommended in PCD have been extrapolated from cystic fibrosis (CF) and non-CF bronchiectasis literature. Mainstays of therapy are reviewed in detail, and should include at a minimum: regular airway clearance, routine microbiological surveillance, antibiotic treatment for pulmonary exacerbation, and health vaccinations.

evidence.reference_title: Antimicrobial prophylaxis for primary immunodeficiencies.
evidence.snippet: Antibiotic prophylaxis is one of the mainstays of therapy of primary immunodeficiencies.

nodes.description: Defects in the structure and function of motile cilia impair mucociliary clearance.

evidence.snippet: A rare, genetically heterogeneous, primarily respiratory disorder characterized by chronic upper and lower respiratory tract disease.

evidence.reference_title: [Primary ciliary dyskinesia. Ciliopathies].
evidence.snippet: Primary ciliary dyskinesia is a genetically inherited syndrome characterized by cilia immotility or dysmotility. Deficiency in mucociliary clearance produces chronic respiratory infections since birth.

evidence.reference_title: Cilia dysfunction.
evidence.snippet: Ciliary dysfunction may be primary, the result of genetic mutations resulting in abnormal cilia structure, or secondary, the result of environmental, infectious or inflammatory stimuli that disrupt normal motility or coordination.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic defects in ciliary structure and function.
evidence.snippet: The most prominent genetic abnormality involving motile cilia (and the respiratory tract) is primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD). PCD is a rare, usually autosomal recessive, genetically heterogeneous disorder characterized by sino-pulmonary disease, laterality defects, and male infertility.

evidence.reference_title: Mislocalization of DNAH5 and DNAH9 in respiratory cells from patients with primary ciliary dyskinesia.
evidence.snippet: Primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD) is a genetically heterogeneous disorder characterized by recurrent infections of the airways and situs inversus in half of the affected offspring. The most frequent genetic defects comprise recessive mutations of DNAH5 and DNAI1, which encode outer dynein arm (ODA) components.

evidence.reference_title: A nonsense variant in NME5 causes human primary ciliary dyskinesia with radial spoke defects.
evidence.snippet: Primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD) is a genetically heterogeneous disorder characterized by defects in the function or structure of motitle cilia
DNAH5 | dynein axonemal heavy chain 5 | hgnc:2950 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

evidence.reference_title: A Japanese Case of Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia with DNAH5 Mutations.
evidence.snippet: A genetic examination detected compound heterozygous mutations of DNAH5 that encode ODA components.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic variants in CLXN encoding the outer dynein arm docking-associated calcium-binding protein calaxin cause primary ciliary dyskinesia.
evidence.snippet: Most PCD-causing variants result in abnormal outer dynein arms (ODAs), which provide the generative force for respiratory ciliary beating and proper mucociliary clearance.
DNAI1 | dynein axonemal intermediate chain 1 | hgnc:2954 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in DNAI1 (IC78) cause primary ciliary dyskinesia.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in DNAI1 (IC78) cause primary ciliary dyskinesia.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic variants identified using whole-exome sequencing in Chinese patients with primary ciliary dyskinesia.
evidence.snippet: 13 rare variants were identified in patients with PCD, among which were three homozygous causative variants (including one splicing variant) in the PCD-associated genes CCDC40 and DNAI1.

evidence.reference_title: Mislocalization of DNAH5 and DNAH9 in respiratory cells from patients with primary ciliary dyskinesia.
evidence.snippet: The most frequent genetic defects comprise recessive mutations of DNAH5 and DNAI1, which encode outer dynein arm (ODA) components.

evidence.reference_title: Axonemal dynein intermediate-chain gene (DNAI1) mutations result in situs inversus and primary ciliary dyskinesia (Kartagener syndrome).
evidence.snippet: We identified compound heterozygous DNAI1 gene defects in three independent patients and in two of their siblings who presented with PCD and situs solitus.

nodes.description: Failure to effectively remove mucus and pathogens from respiratory passages.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology and treatment of airway mucociliary clearance. A moving tale.
evidence.snippet: Failure to keep the airways sterile by MCC results in a host inflammatory response to the persistent microorganisms which, if it becomes chronic, causes damage to the airway wall and upregulation of mucus production manifest clinically as bronchiectasis, sinusitis and otitis.

evidence.reference_title: Primary ciliary dyskinesia and newborn respiratory distress.
evidence.snippet: Primary ciliary dyskinesia is an autosomal recessive genetic disease that results in impaired mucociliary clearance causing progressive involvement of the upper and lower respiratory tract, characterized by airway obstruction and recurrent infections of the lungs, middle ear and paranasal sinuses.

evidence.reference_title: Cilia and disease.
evidence.snippet: Disruption of 9+2 cilia, which move mucus across respiratory epithelia, leads to rhinitis, sinusitis and bronchiectasis.

evidence.reference_title: Olfactory dysfunction is worse in primary ciliary dyskinesia compared with other causes of chronic sinusitis in children.
evidence.snippet: We hypothesised that olfactory function could be impaired in primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD). Olfaction, nasal nitric oxide (nNO) and sinus CT were assessed in patients with PCD and non-PCD sinus disease, and healthy controls (no CT scan). PCD and non-PCD patients had similar severity of sinus disease.

evidence.reference_title: Respiratory Aspects of Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia.
evidence.snippet: This review article explores the respiratory aspects of primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD), a rare, heterogenous, genetic disorder characterized by impaired motile ciliary function. It discusses the clinical diagnosis and management strategies for PCD-related respiratory disease, including chronic sinusitis, otitis media with effusion, recurrent pneumonia, and bronchiectasis.

evidence.reference_title: In vitro measurement of ciliary beat frequency in 92 children with recurrent respiratory tract problems.
evidence.snippet: Ninety-two children with chronic respiratory symptoms were divided into 4 groups: 18 children with refractory asthma, 10 with bronchiectasis without dextrocardia, 18 with dextrocardia and 46 with recurrent respiratory tract infections.

evidence.reference_title: Prevalence of chronic rhinosinusitis in bronchiectasis patients suspected of ciliary dyskinesia.
evidence.snippet: The objective of this study was to evaluate the prevalence of chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) and its characteristics in bronchiectasis patients suspected of harboring ciliary dyskinesia.

evidence.reference_title: Simultaneous sinus and lung infections in patients with primary ciliary dyskinesia.
evidence.snippet: The sinuses should be considered as a bacterial reservoir and a target for surgery and antibiotic treatment in patients with primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD).
